posts.hook: Why some men literally cannot see red and green — the biology of color blindness.
posts.caption:
Color vision deficiency isn't just seeing gray.

The most common type: red-green deficiency.

How it works:
Your retina has 3 types of cone cells — for red, green, and blue wavelengths.

In deuteranomaly (most common): green cones detect the wrong wavelength.

Result: red and green appear similar — both brownish-yellow.

This affects job performance in:
- Design
- Electrical work (colored wires)
- Food inspection
- Military
- Medicine (blood reading)

Free 2-minute test: axeo.vision

#ColorVision #RedGreenBlind #EyeHealth #Axeo #AxeoVision #Optometry #VisionScience

Each pulses with its color. Then 'M cone' arrow shifts — its detection peak overlaps with 'L cone'. Text: 'Deuteranomaly — green cone shifted'. Teal medical outlines on dark navy background. PART 2 (10s): Color wheel comparison — LEFT labeled 'Normal vision', RIGHT labeled 'Deuteranomaly'. The red and green sectors appear as similar brownish-yellow on the right. Gradual animation. PART 3 (8s): Three real-world icons — traffic light, electrical wires, food plate — each shown in normal vs color-blind simulation. Hard cuts. 9:16. 28 seconds.

posts.hook: 1 in 12 men has a color vision deficiency. Most found out by accident.
posts.caption:
Color vision deficiency is one of the most under-screened conditions in ophthalmology.

→ 8% of men affected globally
→ 0.5% of women affected
→ Inherited on the X chromosome
→ No cure — but testing reveals it

Knowing matters:
- Career choices that require color discrimination
- Driving safety (signal color recognition)
- Understanding your child's school screening results

Free Ishihara-style color test at axeo.vision — 12 plates, 2 minutes.

posts.hook: Color blindness isn't seeing in black and white — here's what it actually looks like.
posts.caption:
Most people think color blindness = grayscale.

That's almost never true.

The reality:

→ Deuteranopia: Red and green look the same — brownish
→ Protanopia: Red appears very dark or black
→ Tritanopia: Blue-yellow confusion (rarest)
→ Monochromacy: True grayscale — affects 1 in 30,000

The most common type (deuteranomaly) is so mild many people don't notice until tested.

Free Ishihara test at axeo.vision — 2 minutes.

#ColorBlindness #ColorVision #EyeHealth #Axeo #Optometry #VisionScience
posts.visual_notes: Color simulation comparison cards for each type. Real-world scene shown in normal vs each deficiency type. Clear labeling. Educational, non-alarmist.

posts.hook: Color vision deficiency affects 300 million people worldwide. Most don't know they have it. A thread on what you're actually missing:
posts.caption:
1/ 1 in 12 men and 1 in 200 women have some form of color vision deficiency. The most common type is deuteranomaly — a shift in green cone sensitivity.

2/ It's not seeing in grayscale. Most people with color deficiency see most colors — but red and green appear similar: a brownish yellow that makes discrimination impossible.

3/ It's inherited on the X chromosome. Men have one X, so one defective gene is enough. Women have two X chromosomes — both must carry the gene for full deficiency.

4/ Careers affected: pilots, train drivers, electricians, designers, surgeons, military — all have color vision requirements.

5/ There's no cure. But color vision glasses (EnChroma etc.) can help some people with specific deficiency types distinguish more colors.

6/ The Ishihara test has been the gold standard since 1917. You can take a free version (12 plates) at axeo.vision in 2 minutes.

Share this for the 1 in 12 men who might not know.
posts.visual_notes: Text thread. Optional: Ishihara plate graphic. Educational, shareable format.

posts.hook: Found out at 32 that I've had mild color vision deficiency my whole life — how to test yourself at home
posts.caption:
r/colorblind or r/optometry

I failed a color vision screening at a job application (aircraft maintenance) at age 32. Had never been tested before — not in school, not at any eye appointment.

Turns out I have deuteranomaly, the mild form of red-green deficiency.

Looking back, so many things made sense: why I struggled with certain data visualizations, why I'd occasionally misread resistor color codes, why 'khaki' and 'olive' looked identical to me.

Mild deuteranomaly is incredibly common — approximately 5% of men. Many people live their whole lives not knowing.

If you want to get a baseline, there are free online options. The one I found most comprehensive is axeo.vision (12 Ishihara-style plates, no signup, browser-based). It's not a clinical replacement but it'll give you a strong indication.

Happy to answer questions about what the diagnosis process looks like if anyone is curious.
posts.visual_notes: Authentic first-person format. Soft promotion through experience. r/colorblind community-appropriate tone.
